elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate orbifolds of lattice conformal field theories with the goal of constructing theories with large gap.
elements.sent-2411-002: We consider Barnes-Wall lattices, which are a family of lattices with no short vectors, and orbifold by an extraspecial 2-group of lattice automorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-003: To construct the orbifold CFT, we investigate the orbifold vertex operator algebra and its twisted modules.
elements.sent-2411-004: To obtain a holomorphic CFT, a certain anomaly 3-cocycle $\omega$ needs to vanish; based on evidence we provide, we conjecture that it indeed does.
elements.sent-2411-005: Granting this conjecture, we construct a holomorphic CFT of central charge 128 with gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this article we consider orbifolds of lattice conformal field theories.
elements.sent-2411-007: Motivated by $AdS_3/CFT_2$ holography, sparseness conditions such as \cite{Hartman:2014oaa , and the proposal of extremal CFTs \cite{MR2388095, Witten:2007kt , our goal is to construct theories with large gaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: That is, we want to construct CFTs whose lightest non-vacuum primary fields have relatively large weight.
elements.sent-2411-009: How to construct orbifold CFTs is of course well known to physicists.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, some of the steps involve important subtleties and need to be done carefully and mathematically rigorously.
elements.sent-2411-011: Let us therefore start out with a very brief summary of how CFTs and their orbifolds are described mathematically by the theory of vertex operator algebras (VOAs) \cite{LL .}
elements.sent-2411-012: A VOA $V$ describes the symmetry algebra of a 2d CFT, that is the set of its holomorphic (or anti-holomorphic) fields.
elements.sent-2411-013: The irreducible modules (or representations) $W_i$ of $V$ are the primary fields of the CFT.
elements.sent-2411-014: A rational VOA has a finite number of such modules.
elements.sent-2411-015: A holomorphic VOA only has a single module, namely $V$ itself.
elements.sent-2411-016: It is therefore by itself already a holomorphic (or chiral) CFT, that is a CFT whose fields are all holomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-017: For a more general rational VOA we construct a CFT by taking a modular invariant combination of holomorphic and anti-holomorphic modules.
elements.sent-2411-018: That is, given a modular invariant $N_{ij}$, we construct a full CFT whose space of states is given by \be \cH = \bigoplus_{h,\bar h \cH_{(h,\bar h) = \bigoplus_{i,j N_{ij W_i \otimes \bar W_j\ .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In general, given a VOA $V$, there are multiple modular invariants leading to different CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-020: The ADE classification of the Virasoro minimal models \cite{Cappelli:1986hf,Cappelli:1987xt,Kato:1987td is probably the best known example of this.}
elements.sent-2411-021: For a mathematical description of this program of constructing full CFTs, especially in the context of orbifold theories, see \cite{MR4256405 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: To construct orbifolds of $V$, we pick an automorphism group $G$ of $V$ and consider the sub-VOA $V^G$, that is the set of states in $V$ invariant under $G$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Its irreducible modules can be obtained from the twisted sectors, that is the twisted modules of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-024: We can then again construct a CFT from modular invariant combinations of the modules of $V^G$.
elements.sent-2411-025: One construction that always works is to choose the diagonal modular invariant.
elements.sent-2411-026: This leads to a CFT whose states are invariant when $G$ acts simultaneously on the left- and right-movers.
elements.sent-2411-027: To put it another way, this is a left-right-symmetric orbifold.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, even if $V$ is holomorphic, this orbifold is no longer holomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-029: To construct a holomorphic orbifold CFT, it is necessary to find another modular invariant.
elements.sent-2411-030: With this general summary out of the way, let us define the gap of a CFT.
elements.sent-2411-031: First take the case where $V$ is a holomorphic VOA.
elements.sent-2411-032: The gap of $V$ is then the smallest conformal weight $\mu_V$ of any non-vacuum Virasoro highest weight vector in $V$\cite{Hohn:2019luz .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Modularity of the partition function gives the bound $\mu_V\leq \lfloor \frac c{24}\rfloor +1$ \cite{MR1614941 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: An extremal VOA (or CFT) is a holomorphic VOA that saturates that inequality \cite{MR1614941, MR2388095, Witten:2007kt .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The most famous example of an extremal VOA is the Monster VOA $V^\natural$ \cite{MR843307,MR996026 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Other extremal VOAs are known up to $c=40$, but no examples for higher central charge are known.
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, no holomorphic VOA with $\mu_V > 2$ is known.
elements.sent-2411-039: Next consider non-holomorphic VOAs and their CFTs $\cH$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Here the gap is the smallest total conformal weight $h+\bar h$ of any non-vacuum vector in $\cH$ that is highest weight with respect to both the holomorphic and the anti-holomorphic Virasoro algebra $Vir\otimes \overline{Vir}$.
elements.sent-2411-041: In the special case when $V$ is holomorphic, $\cH= V\otimes \bar V$ and the gap is simply given by $\mu_V$ as above.
elements.sent-2411-042: So far, no full CFT with gap greater than 2 is known.
elements.sent-2411-043: The goal of this article is to construct such CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-044: To this end we construct orbifolds of Barnes-Wall lattice VOAs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Barnes-Wall lattices $BW(m)$ are a family of even lattices that are unimodular for $m$ odd \cite{MR0106893,MR335654,GriessRobertL2011Aitg .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Because their shortest vectors are relatively long and their automorphism groups are big, they are suitable candidates for constructing CFTs with large gap.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this article we obtain two main results: By orbifolding the Barnes-Wall lattice in $d=128$ by the extraspecial group $E(7)$, we first construct a CFT of central charge $c=128$ and gap 2 that only has a single highest weight vector of weight 2.
elements.sent-2411-048: To our knowledge this is the CFT with the fewest such states known in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-049: Second, we conjecture that we can use another modular invariant of this orbifold to construct a holomorphic CFT of central charge $c=128$ with gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-050: This construction only works if a certain anomaly 3-cocycle $\omega$ in $H^3(G,U(1))$ is trivial.
elements.sent-2411-051: We check various necessary conditions for the vanishing of $\omega$, providing evidence for the conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our construction is somewhat lengthy and technical, but fundamentally proceeds along the lines of the construction of the Monster VOA $V^\natural$ in \cite{MR996026 , that is the extremal VOA with $c=24$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: As a roadmap let us therefore sketch the steps in their construction and compare them to our steps.
elements.sent-2411-054: Their starting point is the VOA $V_L$ of the Leech lattice \cite{MR209983 , the even unimodular extremal lattice in $d=24$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The Leech lattice VOA has character \be \Tr_{V_L q^{L_0 = 1+ 24 q + O(q^2)\ .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The Leech lattice is extremal, which means it has no vectors of length squared 2.
elements.sent-2411-057: The lattice states thus only contribute at order $q^2$ and higher.
elements.sent-2411-058: Next, to eliminate the states of weight 1, they orbifold by the lattice automorphism $z$ that maps $\vec x \mapsto -\vec x$.
elements.sent-2411-059: The character of the invariant sub-VOA is thus \be \Tr_{V_L^{\Z_2 q^{L_0 = 1 + O(q^2)\ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: To turn it into one, they adjoin a module coming from the $z$-twisted module to construct $V^{orb(\Z_2) = V^\natural$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The twisted module has high enough vacuum anomaly that it does not introduce any new states of weight 1.
elements.sent-2411-062: The character of $V^\natural$ is then famously \be \Tr_{V_L^{\natural q^{L_0 = 1 + 196884 q^2 + O(q^3)\ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We want to mimic this construction to obtain CFTs with gaps larger than 2.
elements.sent-2411-064: In lieu of the Leech lattice we use Barnes-Wall lattices $BW(m)$, a family of lattices of dimension $2^m$.
elements.sent-2411-065: For odd $m\geq3$, they are even and unimodular.
elements.sent-2411-066: For our purposes, they have two important properties: First, their shortest vectors have relatively big length squared, namely $2^{\lfloor m/2\rfloor}$.
elements.sent-2411-068: For $m=5$, $BW(5)$ is extremal, which for $d=32$ simply means that there are no vectors of length squared 2.
elements.sent-2411-069: Some of the orbifolds in those two cases were studied in \cite{Gemunden:2019dtr,Burbano:2021loy and \cite{Moller:2024plb respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Even though for larger $m$ the Barnes-Wall lattices are no longer extremal, they still mimic the Leech lattice's property of not having very short vectors.
elements.sent-2411-071: For our construction we specifically use the $d=128=2^7$ BW lattice.
elements.sent-2411-072: Its lattice VOA has character \be \Tr_{V_L q^{L_0 = 1+128 q+8384 q^2+374272 q^3+O(q^4) \ .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The automorphism group $BRW(m)$ contains as a subgroup an extraspecial 2-group $2_+^{2m+1}$, which we will denote by $E(m)$; the full automorphism group is in fact an index 2 subgroup of the normalizer of $E(m)$ in the orthogonal group.
elements.sent-2411-074: In lieu of $\Z_2$, we orbifold by this larger, non-Abelian group $E(7)$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The reason for this is the following.
elements.sent-2411-076: To achieve a large gap CFT, we ask two things of an orbifold group: First, we want it to project out all the light non-Virasoro descendant states of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-077: As we will see, $E(m)$ is powerful enough to do this up to weight 4.
elements.sent-2411-079: The smallest weight of a Virasoro highest weight state is thus indeed 4.
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally we would like to add back modules to $V_L^{E(7)}$ to turn it back into a holomorphic VOA.
elements.sent-2411-081: To obtain such irreducible modules of a fixed point VOA $V^{G}$, we can first construct the twisted modules of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-082: A $g$-twisted module $W_g$ then carries a projective representation $\phi_g(\cdot)$ of the centralizer $C_G(g)$ of the orbifold group $G$ \cite{MR1794264, MR2023933,MR3715704 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: That is, $\phi_g(\cdot)$ satisfies \be \phi_g(h_1)\phi_g(h_2)=c_g(h_1,h_2)\phi_g(h_1h_2)\ , \ee where the multiplier $c_g(\cdot, \cdot)$ is given by a 2-cocycle in the Schur multiplier $H^2(G,U(1))$.
elements.sent-2411-084: All irreducible modules of $V^G$ can then be obtained from the twisted modules of $V$ by projecting to irreducible representations of $\phi_g(\cdot)$\cite{MR3715704, MR2040864 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Last, we want to make sure that the modules we adjoin do not reintroduce any states of weight less than 4.
elements.sent-2411-086: To ensure this, we require that all twisted sectors have a vacuum anomaly of at least 4.
elements.sent-2411-087: This is the second thing we ask of our orbifold group.
elements.sent-2411-088: As we will see in section~\ref{s:BWEm , for $m\geq 7$ $E(m)$ has this property.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This means that if we can add back twisted modules to obtain a holomorphic VOA, that VOA will have gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-090: However, as mentioned above, this is possible only if a certain anomaly 3-cocycle $\omega$ vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-091: Let us discuss this and other issues next.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the previous section we gave an outline of the steps of our construction.
elements.sent-2411-093: As mentioned in the beginning however, several of these steps are mathematically quite subtle and need to be done carefully.
elements.sent-2411-094: Let us therefore go over the procedure outlined above more carefully.
elements.sent-2411-095: In particular we want to point out three issues that we need to deal with.
elements.sent-2411-096: The first issue is that to orbifold $V_L$, we need to work with automorphisms of the VOA $V_L$, and not of the lattice $L$.
elements.sent-2411-097: To find such automorphisms, we need to lift automorphisms of $L$ to automorphisms of $V_L$, or equivalently to automorphisms of the twisted group algebra of $L$ \cite{MR1745258 .}
elements.sent-2411-098: For a given automorphism $g$ of $L$ we can always do this to obtain an automorphism $\hat g$ of $V_L$.
elements.sent-2411-099: However, there is no guarantee that the $\hat g$ will satisfy the same group relations as the $g$.
elements.sent-2411-100: A typical example of this is order doubling, in which case the order of $\hat g$ is twice the order of $g$ \cite{vanEkeren2017, Harvey:2017rko .}
elements.sent-2411-101: What this means is that the $\hat g$ will no longer generate the group $G$, but rather a cover of it.
elements.sent-2411-102: In principle such a cover is a perfectly good group of automorphisms of $V_L$ that could be used for orbifolding.
elements.sent-2411-103: For our purposes however having a cover is bad: if there are multiple preimages of the identity, then those will give light twisted sector vacuum anomalies, which we want to avoid.
elements.sent-2411-104: We therefore need to make sure that our extraspecial group $E(m)$ lifts to $E(m)$; that is, we need to ensure that the lifted automorphisms still satisfy the relations of the extraspecial group.
elements.sent-2411-105: Theorem~\ref{thm:Elift in section~\ref{s:BWEm shows that this is the case, so that $E(m)$ is indeed a group of automorphisms of $V_L$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: This brings us to the second issue.
elements.sent-2411-107: The result above allows us to work with $E(m)$ as a group of automorphisms of $V_L$.
elements.sent-2411-108: In particular we can construct $V_L^{E(m)}$ and its modules from the twisted modules $W_g$ of our lattice VOA.
elements.sent-2411-109: For this we use the fact that for lattice VOAs, the twisted modules $W_g$ can be constructed as induced representations of a smaller representation $\rho$ on $\Omega_0$, the so called defect representation \cite{MR820716, MR2172171 .}
elements.sent-2411-110: To then obtain the actual irreducible modules and their characters, we need to construct the projective representation $\phi_g(\cdot)$ of $C_G(g)$ on $W_g$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Even though we know for general reasons that the $\phi_g(\cdot)$ exist abstractly, computing them concretely takes some work.
elements.sent-2411-112: We do this by first restricting the action of $\phi_g(\cdot)$ to $\Omega_0$.
elements.sent-2411-113: The restricted representation we then obtain by constructing intertwiner maps $T_h$: We use the fact that for an automorphism $h\in C_G(g)$, $\rho$ and $\rho \circ h$ are projectively equivalent, that is related by an isomorphism $T_h$.
elements.sent-2411-114: These $T_h$ then form the sought after restriction of the projective representation of $\phi_g(\cdot)$ to $\Omega_0$.
elements.sent-2411-115: The main computational work here is to find the intertwiner maps $T_h$.
elements.sent-2411-116: In section~\ref{s:defrep we construct these by using Schur averaging, that is by averaging over all elements of $N$ to obtain an $N$-invariant map between $\rho$ and $\rho \circ h$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: However, the order of $N$ grows very quickly in $m$; it tends to be something like $2^{2^m}$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Evaluating the sum even for $m=5$ or 7 becomes very cumbersome.
elements.sent-2411-119: We therefore describe a method that reduces the summation to a linear algebra problem in a $2^m$ dimensional vector space over $\F_2$.
elements.sent-2411-120: This problem can then be solved in reasonable time by standard computer algebra systems such as Magma or Mathematica.
elements.sent-2411-121: This allows us to construct the intertwining representation $T_h$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In principle we could then proceed to lift the representation $T_h$ on $\Omega_0$ to the representation $\phi_g(\cdot)$ on $W_g$.
elements.sent-2411-123: In particular this would allow us to compute the characters of all the irreducible modules of the orbifold.
elements.sent-2411-125: Instead, for reasons explained below, we focus on the multiplier $c_g(\cdot,\cdot)$ of the representation, which we can obtain by comparing a single entry of the products of intertwiner matrices.
elements.sent-2411-126: Having constructed the twisted modules, we first use the diagonal modular invariant to construct CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-127: For $m=7$, this is the content of proposition~\ref{prop:diag .}
elements.sent-2411-128: Unfortunately the resulting CFT still has only gap 2.
elements.sent-2411-129: The reason for this is that it contains the state $a_{-1}\cdot \bar a_{-1}\vac$ which is invariant under the diagonal action of the orbifold group.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, this is the only highest weight state of this weight, meaning there is only one primary of total weight 2.
elements.sent-2411-131: To get around this problem we investigate holomorphic extensions of $V_L^{E(7)}$.
elements.sent-2411-132: As argued above, if such an extension exists, up to order 4 its character is the same as (\ref{charVE7 ).}
elements.sent-2411-133: This would mean that we have indeed constructed a holomorphic CFT with gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-134: This however brings us to the third issue, namely if we can find a holomorphic extension of $V_L^{E(7)}$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Because $V_L^{E(7)}$ is strongly rational, its modules form a modular tensor category \cite{MR2387861,MR2468370 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: This modular tensor category is given by a twisted Drinfeld double of the group $G$, $D^\omega(G)$-mod \cite{dijkgraaf1990,Roche:1990hs .}
elements.sent-2411-138: By the work of \cite{Evans:2018qgz , finding a holomorphic extension $V^{orb(G)}$ is only possible $\omega$ if is trivial.}
elements.sent-2411-139: In physics $\omega$ is sometimes called the 't Hooft anomaly.
elements.sent-2411-140: Its relevance for holomorphic orbifolds was discussed in \cite{FV87 .}
elements.sent-2411-141: We conjecture that for the orbifold of the $d=128$ BW lattice VOA by $E(7)$, $\omega$ is trivial.
elements.sent-2411-142: We will not prove this here, but we do provide two important pieces of evidence in favor of this conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-143: The first piece of evidence comes from restricting $\omega$ to cyclic subgroups of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-144: There is a simple criterion for when the restriction of $\omega$ to a cyclic subgroup is trivial: \cite{vanEkeren2017 call this the `type 0 condition', and the physics literature calls it `level matching' \cite{Vafa:1986wx .}}
elements.sent-2411-145: We check that this condition is satisfied for our $E(7)$ orbifold.
elements.sent-2411-146: The second piece of evidence comes from considering the multipliers $c_g(\cdot,\cdot)$ of the twisted module representations $\phi_g(\cdot)$.
elements.sent-2411-147: These 2-cocycles are related to $\omega$ via \cite{Evans:2018qgz \be c_g(h_1,h_2) = \omega(g,h_1,h_2)\omega(h_1,h_2, h_2^{-1 h_1^{-1 gh_1h_2)\omega(h_1,h_1^{-1 gh_1,h_2)^* \ee In particular, if $\omega$ is trivial, necessarily all multipliers $c_g(\cdot,\cdot)$ also need to be trivial.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: That is, the projective representations of the centralizers $C_G(g)$ on the $g$-twisted modules are actually (after an appropriate choice of section) linear representations.
elements.sent-2411-149: We check that for our $E(7)$ orbifold, all $c_g(\cdot,\cdot)$ are trivial, providing our second piece of evidence in favor of the conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-150: Granting that $\omega$ is trivial, there is indeed a holomorphic extension of $V^{E(7)}$.
elements.sent-2411-151: This in turn implies that we can construct a holomorphic CFT of central charge $c=128$ that has gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-152: To our knowledge this is the first example of a CFT in two dimensions with gap larger than 2.
elements.sent-2411-153: As such, it is of interest in physics for instance in the context of $AdS_3/CFT_2$ holography, where having a sparse light spectrum is desirable \cite{Hartman:2014oaa .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Even though our CFT is not extremal, it has fewer light states than theories previously constructed from lattice orbifolds \cite{Gemunden:2018mkh,Gemunden:2019dtr or permutation orbifolds \cite{Belin:2014fna,Haehl:2014yla,Belin:2015hwa,Keller:2017rtk .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Outside of holography, it can be used to test conjectures such as \cite{Collins:2022nux .}
elements.sent-2411-156: This article is structured in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-157: In section~2 we discuss some background on the construction of VOAs from lattices and orbifolds.
elements.sent-2411-158: In section~3 we introduce the Barnes-Wall lattices, their automorphism groups, the extraspecial 2-group $E(m)$ and its properties, and in particular its lift to automorphisms of the VOA.
elements.sent-2411-159: In section~4 we introduce the intertwining representation.
elements.sent-2411-160: We explain how to compute the intertwiner matrices explicitly and check that the 2-cocycle vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-161: In section~5 we then return to the construction of orbifold VOAs and apply these results to construct CFTs with large gaps.
elements.sent-2411-162: Let us start with a summary of the mathematical theory of VOA orbifolds.
elements.sent-2411-163: Let $V$ be a strongly rational VOA, that is rational, $C_2$-cofinite, simple, self-contragredient and of CFT-type.
elements.sent-2411-164: The significance of this is that \cite{MR2387861 established that the fusion rules for the modules of a strongly rational VOA satisfy the Verlinde formula \cite{Verlinde:1988sn and hence that the modules form a modular tensor category \cite{MR2468370 .}}}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Lattice VOAs and twisted group algebras
elements.sent-2411-195: Let us apply the above to the concrete example of lattice VOAs.
elements.sent-2411-196: Given a positive definite even lattice $L$, we can construct an associated strongly rational VOA \cite{MR843307,MR996026 .}
elements.sent-2411-197: The main ingredient for this is the twisted group algebra.
elements.sent-2411-198: The twisted group algebra $\C_\epsilon[L]=:\hat L$ has multiplication $e_{\alpha e_{\beta =\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)e_{\alpha+\beta}$ where the 2-cocycle $\epsilon$ evaluated on the basis vectors is $\epsilon(\alpha_i,\alpha_j)= (-1)^{H_{ij }$, where $H$ is the `half-Gram matrix' defined as \be H_{ij = \left\{ \begin{array {cc G_{ij : i>j G_{ij /2 : i=j 0 :i<j \end{array \right .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Given an automorphism $g\in Aut(L)$, define the bilinear form $B_g$ as \be B_g(\alpha_i,\alpha_j) = \left\{ \begin{array {cc H(\alpha_i,\alpha_j) - H(\alpha_ig,\alpha_jg) : i>j 0 :i\leq j \end{array \right.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The map $\hat g$ acting on vectors $e_\alpha$ in $\C_\epsilon[L]$ as \be \hat g (e_\alpha) = u_g(\alpha)e_{\alpha g \ee is then an automorphism of the twisted lattice algebra, $\hat g \in Aut(\hat L)$.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Finally, $\hat g$ is called a standard lift if $u_g=1$ on the fixed point lattice $L_g$\cite{MR820716 .}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Twisted modules and the defect representation
elements.sent-2411-202: We now want to construct the $\hat g$-twisted modules of the lattice VOA $V_L$.
elements.sent-2411-203: For standard lifts $\hat g$, the twisted modules $W_{\hat g}$ for lattice VOAs were constructed in \cite{MR820716, MR2172171 as induced representations of a smaller representation $\Omega_0$ of a finite group, the so called defect representation.}
elements.sent-2411-204: We will briefly review this construction, using the notation of \cite{ MR2172171 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: We will then also explain the idea behind constructing the projective representation $\phi_{\hat g}$.
elements.sent-2411-206: Twisted modules of non-standard lifts can be constructed similarly using \cite{MR1372724 .}
elements.sent-2411-207: As we will see in section~\ref{s:orbifolds however, all our lifts turn out to be standard lifts, so that we will only describe the standard case in what follows.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Let $g\in Aut(L)$ and $\hat g$ its lift to $Aut(V_L)$.
elements.sent-2411-209: Define the group \be N:= L^\perp_g/L(1-g) \ee where $L^\perp_g$ is the lattice of points in $L$ orthogonal to the fixed point lattice $L_g$.
elements.sent-2411-210: To construct it explicitly, define $D:=1-g$ and find its Smith Normal Form \be S = PD Q \ , \qquad S = \diag(s_1,s_2,\ldots, s_d,0,\ldots, 0)\ , \ee with $s_i|s_{i+1}$ and $s_i \in \Z_+$.
elements.sent-2411-211: If $\{\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_d,\delta_1,\ldots ,\delta_{\dim L - d}\}$ are the row vectors of $Q^{-1}$, then $N$ is generated by the $[\alpha_i]$ and has the form \be N \cong \bigoplus_{i=1 ^k (\Z / s_i\Z)\ .}
elements.sent-2411-212: We now construct a projective representation $(\rho,\Omega_0)$ of $N$ whose 2-cocycle has skew $C$ following \cite{MR2742735 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: The idea of the construction is as follows: We pick generators $\alpha_i, \beta_i$ of $N$ which are orthogonal to each other except for $C(\alpha_i,\beta_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-214: We then interpret the $\alpha_i$ as generators of some subgroup $A<N$, and the $\beta_i$ as generators of $\check A$, the group of irreducible characters of $A$, giving an isomorphism \be \gamma: N \to A+\check A \qquad [\mu] \mapsto (x_{[\mu] ,\chi_{[\mu] )\ .}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Moreover this representation is irreducible, and the unique irreducible representation with this 2-cocycle \cite{MR3299063 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: We can now consider the action of an element $\hat h$ in the centralizer of $\hat g$ on $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-217: We have \be \rho_{\hat h (\alpha) := u_h(\alpha) \rho \circ h (\alpha) =u_h(\alpha) \rho(\alpha h)\ .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Because their skew is the same, we have that $P_{\hat h (\cdot,\cdot)/P(\cdot, \cdot)$ is symmetric.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Moreover for the automorphisms $\hat h \in H$ that we consider we find that its diagonal entries vanish.
elements.sent-2411-220: It follows that $P$ and $P_{\hat h}$ are cohomologous --- see section~\ref{ss:coordinate for an explicit construction of the coboundary.}
elements.sent-2411-221: In particular, the 2-cocycle of $\phi_{\hat g}$ is cohomologous to the 2-cocycle of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-222: If we can therefore show that the cocycle $c(\hat h_1,\hat h_2)$ of $T$ is trivial, it will follow that $\phi_{\hat g}$ also has trivial 2-cocycle.
elements.section-2411-003: Barnes-Wall lattices and the extraspecial group
elements.subsection-2411-007: Barnes-Wall lattices and their automorphism groups
elements.sent-2411-223: Let us introduce the main players of our construction, the Barnes-Wall lattices $BW(m)$, their automorphism groups $BRW(m)$ and the extraspecial 2-groups $E(m) = 2_+^{2m+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-224: The Barnes-Wall lattices were constructed in \cite{MR0106893 and independently in \cite{MR335654 ; see \cite{GriessRobertL2011Aitg for an overview.}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: The Barnes-Wall lattices $BW(m)$ are a family of integral lattices of dimension $2^m$.
elements.sent-2411-226: For $m>1$, $BW(m)$ is even, and hence suitable for constructing a VOA.
elements.sent-2411-227: For odd $m\geq 3$, it is unimodular, and for $m$ even its Gram matrix has determinant $2^{2^{m-1 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-228: This means for odd $m\geq 3$ we can use $BW(m)$ to construct holomorphic lattice VOAs.
elements.sent-2411-229: The other important property of Barnes-Wall lattices is that their shortest vectors have length squared $2^{\lfloor m/2\rfloor}$, which makes them suitable for constructing VOAs with large gap.
elements.sent-2411-230: For our computations we will use the simple construction of $BW(m)$ given in \cite{NR02 .}
elements.sent-2411-231: The basic idea is to introduce so-called balanced BW lattices whose generator matrices are given recursively by tensor products.
elements.sent-2411-232: We give detailed expressions for this construction in appendix~\ref{s:BW .}
elements.sent-2411-233: Next let us turn to the automorphism group $BRW(m)=Aut(BW(m))$ first studied in \cite{MR125874,MR142666,MR148736 .}
elements.sent-2411-234: We will first introduce a subgroup of it, namely the extraspecial 2-group $E(m) \simeq 2_+^{2m+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-235: We construct it as a subgroup of the orthogonal group $O(2^m,\R)$ in the following manner.
elements.sent-2411-237: The elements in $E(m)$ are then automorphisms of $BW(m)$.
elements.sent-2411-238: For the full automorphism group $BRW(m)$ we will follow \cite{MR1845897 .}
elements.sent-2411-239: It can be obtained from the normalizer of $E(m)$ in $O(2^m,\R)$.
elements.sent-2411-240: For $m>3$, its structure is \be BRW(m) = E(m).\Omega^+(2m,2)\ .
elements.sent-2411-241: To describe its structure, we first note that $E(m)/Z(E(m))\simeq \F_2^{2m}$ is a vector space over $\F_2$.
elements.sent-2411-242: This vector space has a quadratic form $Q$ defined as $Q(x)=x^2 \in \F_2$, where the square of $x\in E(m)$, which is $\pm\id$, is interpreted as $\pm1 \in \F_2$.
elements.sent-2411-244: The Clifford group is then given by $C_m=E(m).O^+(2m,2)$.
elements.sent-2411-245: By corollary 2.3 of \cite{MR1845897 , it is generated by the following elements of $O(2^m,\R)$: \be \sigma_1\otimes \id_2 \otimes \ldots\otimes \id_2 , \ \sigma_2\otimes \id_2 \otimes \ldots\otimes \id_2 , \ H\ , GL(m,2) ,\ D(m)\ .}
elements.sent-2411-246: That is, the basis vectors of $\R^{2^m}$ are labeled by elements of $F_2^m$ on which elements of $GL(m,2)$ act as bijections, hence giving a permutation of the basis vectors.
elements.sent-2411-247: Moreover we defined \be H = h \otimes\id_2 \otimes \ldots\otimes \id_2\ , \ee with $h=\frac1{\sqrt 2 \begin{pmatrix 1 1 1 -1\end{pmatrix}$ and \be D(m) = diag(1,1,1,-1)\otimes \id_2 \otimes \ldots \otimes \id_2\ .}}
elements.sent-2411-248: It is given by the kernel $\Omega^+(2m,2)$ of the Dickson invariant $D: O^+(2m,2)\to \F_2$, so that indeed $BRW(m)=E(m).\Omega^+(2m,2)$.
elements.sent-2411-249: For a block matrix $U=\begin{pmatrix A B C D\end{pmatrix}\in O^+(2m,2)$ the Dickson invariant is given by $D(U)=\Tr(B^TC)$ \cite{MR85212 .}}

elements.section-2411-005: Extraspecial Orbifolds of Barnes-Wall lattice VOAs
elements.subsection-2411-014: Large gap CFTs from lattice orbifolds
elements.sent-2411-384: Before collecting our results to construct our extraspecial orbifolds, let us give some general considerations for constructing CFTs with large gap from lattice orbifolds.
elements.sent-2411-385: In particular, let us discuss what types of lattices and orbifold groups are appropriate for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-386: The overall goal is to avoid any light states.
elements.sent-2411-387: First we need to ensure that no such states appear from lattice vectors.
elements.sent-2411-388: This means we should work with lattices whose shortest vector has large length.
elements.sent-2411-389: For this we would ideally like to work with extremal lattices whose shortest vectors have length square $2(\lfloor d/24\rfloor+1)$.
elements.sent-2411-390: Unfortunately, such lattices have only been constructed up to $d=80$ \cite{MR209983,MR1662447,MR1489922,MR2999133,MR3225314,MR1985228 , and they are known not to exist if $d>163264$ \cite{MR0376536,MR2854563 .}}
elements.sent-2411-391: For $d=48$ and $d=72$ their orbifolds were considered in \cite{Gemunden:2018mkh,Gemunden:2019dtr , but no CFTs with gap larger than 2 were found.}
elements.sent-2411-392: Barnes-Wall lattices are therefore the next best candidates.
elements.sent-2411-393: For odd $m$ they are even and unimodular, thus giving holomorphic lattice VOAs, and their shortest vectors have length squared $\sqrt{d/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-394: Moreover they have large automorphism groups $BRW(m)$, which becomes important in the next step.
elements.sent-2411-395: Next we need to eliminate light free boson descendants.
elements.sent-2411-396: To this end we want to orbifold by some group $G$ of automorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-398: First, we want to ensure that the twisted modules that are introduced in the orbifold do not produce new light states.
elements.sent-2411-399: To avoid that we want make sure that the vacuum anomalies $\rho_g$ of the $g$-twisted sector is large.
elements.sent-2411-400: As we can see from formula (\ref{vacanomaly ), this constrains the eigenvalues of the elements of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-401: Second, we want to project out as many of the free boson states in the untwisted sector as possible.
elements.sent-2411-402: In the language of section~\ref{ss:Efacts , we want the trivial representation of $G$ to appear as few times as possible in the Heisenberg representation.}
elements.sent-2411-403: On the one hand let us note that if $G$ is Abelian, then \cite{Gemunden:2019dtr \be \dim \Hr_{(2) ^G \geq \frac d2\ , \qquad \dim \Hr_{(3) ^G \geq d\ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-404: On the other hand we can also bound the best possible case.
elements.sent-2411-405: Note that $G<O(d,\R)$, so that any state invariant under the orthogonal group is also invariant under $G$.
elements.sent-2411-406: We want any light state that is invariant to be a Virasoro descendant.
elements.sent-2411-407: In table~\ref{t:Oinv we list the lightest $O(d,\R)$ invariant states and Virasoro descendants.}
elements.sent-2411-408: We see that up to $h=3$ the two match up.
elements.sent-2411-409: Starting at $h=4$ however, there are more invariant states than Virasoro descendants.
elements.sent-2411-410: This means that there is necessarily a Virasoro highest weight state at weight 4.
elements.sent-2411-411: Lattice orbifolds can thus at best produce a VOA of gap 4.
elements.sent-2411-412: This explains why we content ourselves with just the extraspecial group $E(7)$ and did not attempt to consider larger automorphism groups.

elements.sent-2411-413: Finally there is the issue of constructing a CFT from the modules of the VOA $V^G$.
elements.sent-2411-414: We can always choose the diagonal invariant.
elements.sent-2411-415: However, the resulting CFT will always contain a Virasoro primary state of weight $(h,\bar h)=(1,1)$, namely the state $a(-1)\cdot \bar a(-1)\vac$.
elements.sent-2411-416: To avoid this, we can try to construct a holomorphic VOA by finding a modular invariant that extends $V^G$ to a holomorphic VOA $V^{orb(G)}$.
elements.sent-2411-417: The problem here is that this requires the anomaly $\omega\in H^3(G,U(1))$ to be trivial, which imposes constraints on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-418: Let us now apply the results of the previous sections to construct extraspecial orbifolds of Barnes-Wall lattice VOAs.
elements.sent-2411-419: By theorem~\ref{thm:Elift we can lift $E(m)$ to a group of automorphisms of the lattice VOA $V_L$ isomorphic to $E(m)$, allowing us to indeed consider extraspecial orbifolds of $V_L$.}
elements.sent-2411-420: We will focus on the unimodular lattices with $m$ odd.
elements.sent-2411-421: In that case the VOA of the BW lattice is holomorphic and the expressions in section~\ref{ss:oftheory apply.}
elements.sent-2411-422: We will mostly discuss the instances $m=3,5,7$.
elements.sent-2411-423: In principle our methods also apply to higher cases, but obtaining explicit expressions becomes computationally harder and harder.

elements.sent-2411-432: The Heisenberg factor $T_H$ takes into account the contribution of the free boson descendants.
